seoTitle: Add a Likert Scale Grid to Microsoft Forms
metaDescription: Build a compact Likert scale matrix in Microsoft Forms to evaluate multiple statements at once instead of stacking separate choice fields.
intro: Evaluating several related statements with individual multiple-choice questions clutters a survey and confuses respondents. Microsoft Forms' Likert question type packs those statements into a single responsive grid, and this guide shows how to configure it correctly.

steps.heading: Open the extended question menu
steps.body: Click Add New, then use the drop-down arrow next to the standard options to reveal the extended field drawer.

steps.heading: Select the Likert option
steps.body: Choose the Likert question type from the list to drop in a default matrix grid.

steps.heading: Set your overarching prompt
steps.body: Click into the primary question box and enter the overall evaluation prompt for the grid.

steps.heading: Define your scale columns and statements
steps.body: Edit the horizontal option columns for your scale, such as Strongly Disagree to Strongly Agree, then fill in the vertical statement rows you want evaluated.

steps.heading: Expand and require the grid
steps.body: Use Add Statement or Add Option to expand the matrix, and toggle Required so every statement gets a response.

faq.question: How is a Likert grid different from a regular multiple-choice question?
faq.answer: A Likert grid combines several related statements into one compact matrix instead of requiring a separate question for each.

faq.question: Will the grid display properly on a small phone screen?
faq.answer: Yes, but keeping statement text concise helps prevent visual clipping or awkward side-scrolling on mobile.

faq.question: Can I require an answer for every statement in the grid?
faq.answer: Yes, toggling Required ensures respondents provide a score for every row before submitting.

faq.question: How many statements or scale columns can I add?
faq.answer: You can keep adding rows and columns using the Add Statement and Add Option controls, though very large grids can hurt mobile readability.
